description: Causal + structural RTL rules covering coding hygiene, FSMs, CDC/RDC, synthesizability, AI-origin hazards, DFT, and shift-left PPA prediction.

rules.statement: Every signal written inside an always_comb block must be assigned on every control path, typically via defaults at block entry then selective overrides; path-incomplete always_comb infers latches.

prevents.mechanism: Path-complete combinational assignment eliminates inferred latches before synth, avoiding silent CDC/STA hazards downstream.
Default-then-override prevents X retention through latches and reduces RTL/gate divergence.

rules.rationale: Modern lint stacks treat incomplete combinational assignment as a first-order bug class because it creates storage where the reader expects pure logic. AI-generated RTL frequently misses the closing else/default arm; defaulting at block entry is the cheapest defense.

citations.title: Understanding and Mitigating Errors of LLM-Generated RTL Code
Automatically Fix RTL Lint Violations with GenAI

history.rationale: Removed self-defeating per-tool unless ['tool_verilator']: gating a functional rule out of the grading tool disables it for every run (functional-vs-structural corruption).

rules.statement: Sequential blocks must use always_ff with non-blocking assignments (<=); combinational blocks must use always_comb with blocking assignments (=). Mixing assignment operators with the wrong process type creates simulation race conditions and synthesis mismatches. Exceptions require explicit design review and documentation.

prevents.mechanism: Discipline eliminates blocking-in-sequential schedule races and synthesis-vs-sim divergence at the source.
Correct process-type pairing prevents accidental latch inference and synthesis intent ambiguity.

rules.rationale: lowRISC codifies sequential-uses-NBA / combinational-uses-blocking; Verilator keeps dedicated diagnostics for blocking-in-sequential and delayed-combinational misuse because these patterns correlate with hard-to-debug mismatches. Yosys also recommends restricting designs to standard always-block forms. The discipline prevents subtle bugs where blocking assignments in sequential logic create order-dependent behavior within a single time step, causing simulator race conditions that may not manifest until gate-level simulation or silicon. Non-blocking assignments in combinational logic delay updates and break the zero-delay combinational semantics expected by synthesis tools.

history.notes: Original rule was well-structured with good predicts and prevents. Added concrete code examples showing the violation (blocking in always_ff) and compliant version (non-blocking in always_ff). Clarified statement to explicitly mention the assignment operators (<= vs =) and added rationale explaining the technical mechanism of why mixing causes race conditions and synthesis divergence.

rules.statement: Declare every signal explicitly using logic for synthesizable RTL variables and wire for structural connections; enable `default_nettype none at the top of each file to ensure typos and undeclared signals produce compile-time errors instead of silently creating implicit one-bit nets.

prevents.mechanism: Explicit declarations prevent typo-induced floating nets and accidental one-bit truncations from reaching simulation.

rules.rationale: lowRISC explicitly forbids inferred nets and prefers logic over reg/wire for synthesis-facing code, exactly the posture adopted by OpenTitan's lint-clean flow. AI-assisted flows benefit disproportionately because generated code drifts on declaration detail unless a strict type barrier is present. The combination of explicit declarations and `default_nettype none creates a compile-time safety net that catches common errors: misspelled signal names, copy-paste mistakes, and incomplete port connections. This is especially critical in AI-assisted flows where code generation may produce syntactically valid but semantically incorrect signal references. The logic type unifies reg and wire semantics, eliminating confusion about procedural vs continuous assignment contexts.

citations.title: Automatically Fix RTL Lint Violations with GenAI

history.notes: Original rule had good statement and rationale but lacked code examples. Added violating example showing common typo scenario that creates implicit nets, and compliant example demonstrating proper use of `default_nettype none with logic declarations. Enhanced prevents section to cover synthesis width warnings, X-propagation issues, and multi-driver scenarios. Slightly refined statement to clarify wire vs logic usage contexts.

rules.statement: Arithmetic widths, literal widths, and signedness must be explicit at the point of use, especially around adds, shifts, concatenations, compares, and port hookups, since SystemVerilog otherwise permits silent zero/sign-extension or truncation. Use explicit casting ({N{1'b0}, signal}), range selection ([M:N]), or sized literals (8'd42) to make all width conversions visible in the source code.

prevents.mechanism: Explicit widths/signs eliminate WIDTHEXPAND/WIDTHTRUNC warnings at synth and prevent silent datapath truncation bugs.

rules.rationale: Verilator splits width problems into truncation and expansion classes â strong signal that 'works in sim' is not enough for numerically sensitive RTL. Verible and lowRISC make explicit widths a low-friction lint gate. Implicit width conversions are a leading cause of functional bugs in arithmetic datapaths, particularly when mixing signed/unsigned operands or when intermediate results exceed expected bit widths. Explicit width handling makes code review more effective and prevents 'works in simulation but fails in synthesis' scenarios where tool interpretations diverge.

history.notes: Original rule had strong statement and good predicts/prevents, but lacked concrete code examples. Added realistic violating example showing both zero-extension and truncation issues, plus compliant version with explicit width control. Enhanced statement with specific techniques. Added sim and STA prevents entries to complement existing synth prevention.

rules.statement: Each clock/reset domain must commit to a documented reset strategy (synchronous or asynchronous) and polarity (active-high or active-low); if asynchronous assertion is used, deassertion must be locally synchronized per clock domain using a multi-stage synchronizer rather than allowing the async reset signal to float through the fabric.

prevents.mechanism: Synchronized reset deassertion eliminates recovery/removal violations and reset-release metastability classes.
Per-domain reset strategy avoids cross-module polarity bugs and RDC escapes that surface as intermittent lab failures.

rules.rationale: Cummings' reset guidance is still the clearest statement: async reset assertion can be fine, but async removal without a synchronizer is not. Siemens' RDC view brings the modern SoC angle: multiple async reset sources create a verification space CDC checks alone do not cover. Modern RDC tools flag unsynchronized reset deassertion as a distinct hazard class from CDC: the reset release edge can violate recovery/removal timing, cause metastability in the first cycle after reset, and create polarity mismatches when multiple async sources converge. Per-domain synchronization ensures deterministic reset exit and eliminates the need for global reset tree timing closure.

citations.title: Synchronous Resets? Asynchronous Resets? I am so confused...
Navigating Reset Domain Crossings to Safety in Complex SoCs

history.notes: Original rule had strong statement and prevents but lacked code examples. Added violating example showing async reset shared across domains without synchronization, and compliant example with per-domain reset synchronizers. Improved statement to clarify 'multi-stage synchronizer' requirement. Enhanced rationale to explain the distinct RDC hazard class and timing implications.

rules.statement: Decode logic using casex/casez must: (1) assign full output defaults before the case statement to prevent latches, (2) include an explicit default clause that traps illegal conditions, and (3) add assertions that detect X/Z on the selector signal. This prevents RTL-vs-synthesis divergence where X/Z can match wildcard patterns unpredictably.

prevents.mechanism: Explicit defaults and X-trap assertions prevent X-optimism escapes and false decoder hits after reset.

rules.rationale: The SNUG latch-gotchas paper shows how X/Z in the case expression can select the wrong arm under casex/casez, and why pre-case defaults are the safest latch-prevention pattern. lowRISC's prohibition on X-coded RTL behavior and Verilator's CASEINCOMPLETE diagnostics make this rule easy to enforce. Without defaults, incomplete case coverage creates latches that synthesis tools may optimize differently than RTL simulation. X/Z values in casex/casez selectors can match any wildcard bit, causing non-deterministic behavior that differs between RTL (where X may propagate) and gate-level (where X is resolved to 0/1). Explicit assertions catch these conditions early in verification rather than discovering them as intermittent failures post-synthesis.

citations.title: Yet Another Latch and Gotchas Paper

history.notes: Original rule had good statement and rationale but lacked concrete code examples. Added violating example showing casez without defaults/assertions and compliant example with all three required elements. Added two synthesis-stage prevents entries to complement the existing sim-stage prevention. The rule already had appropriate predicts for case_incomplete lint warnings.

rules.statement: Use unique, unique0, or priority only when they match real hardware intent; never use the legacy full_case or parallel_case pragmas — modern keywords add cross-tool checking while the old pragmas cause sim-synth mismatches.

prevents.mechanism: Eliminating full_case/parallel_case pragmas makes RTL intent visible to both simulator and synthesizer, preventing optimized-away enables and silent decoder semantics drift.

rules.rationale: Cummings' 'evil twins' paper remains the canonical warning that comment pragmas can alter synthesis semantics without giving the simulator the same information. VerilogPro and lowRISC show how to keep the benefit of unique/priority while avoiding cargo-culted keyword usage.

citations.title: A Solution to Verilog's full_case parallel_case Evil Twins
SystemVerilog Unique And Priority - How Do I Use Them?

history.notes: Original rule had good statement and rationale with strong citations. Added violating example showing full_case pragma hiding incomplete case from simulator, and compliant example using unique keyword. Added predictions for case_incomplete.count and x_propagation_escapes. Added prevents entries for sim-synth mismatch scenarios that pragmas create.

rules.statement: A synthesizable design signal must have one clear driver and must not participate in combinational feedback unless that loop is a reviewed intentional construct — multi-driven nets and comb loops degrade tool predictability and DFT friendliness.

prevents.mechanism: One-driver discipline prevents synth multidriven warnings, oscillation hazards, and scan/ATPG blockers downstream.

rules.rationale: Verilator explicitly names MULTIDRIVEN, ALWCOMBORDER, and UNOPTFLAT because these constructs degrade simulation clarity and downstream optimization quality. OpenTitan's lint-clean signoff posture makes 'one signal, one obvious owner' a scalable review rule rather than a taste preference. Multi-driven signals create race conditions where assignment order determines the final value, making behavior simulator-dependent and non-deterministic. Combinational loops prevent static timing analysis convergence and block scan-chain insertion. Both constructs force synthesis tools into conservative pessimistic modes that degrade QoR. The one-driver discipline ensures that every signal has a single, unambiguous source of truth, enabling reliable formal verification, ATPG pattern generation, and cross-tool portability.

history.notes: Original rule had strong statement and predictions but lacked code examples. Added realistic multi-driver and combinational loop violation examples. Enhanced rationale to explain race conditions, STA impact, and DFT implications. Added prevents entries for sim races, DFT scan coverage, and STA convergence to quantify downstream impact beyond the existing synth warning prevention.

rules.statement: Every nontrivial FSM must declare its intended encoding explicitly using synthesis attributes (e.g., (* fsm_encoding = "one_hot" *), (* fsm_encoding = "binary" *), or (* fsm_encoding = "gray" *)) rather than leaving the choice to synthesis tool defaults—explicit encoding makes synthesis intent testable and ensures consistent implementation across tools.

prevents.mechanism: Declared encoding makes the synthesis FSM-extraction outcome predictable and reviewable rather than tool-dependent.

rules.rationale: lowRISC requires enum-based FSM coding; AMD exposes explicit fsm_extraction controls because encoding style changes the implementation, not just the look. VerilogPro's one-hot examples reinforce that encoding choice must be visible at source level. Without explicit encoding declarations, different synthesis tools may choose different FSM implementations (binary vs one-hot vs gray) based on heuristics, leading to unpredictable area/timing tradeoffs and making cross-tool verification difficult. Explicit encoding also enables automated checking that the synthesized netlist matches the designer's intent, preventing silent optimization changes that could affect power or timing.

history.notes: Original rule had good statement and predictions but lacked code examples. Added realistic FSM examples showing violation (no encoding attribute) vs compliance (explicit fsm_encoding attribute). Enhanced statement to specify synthesis attribute syntax. Added three prevents entries covering timing, power, and coverage impacts of undeclared encoding. Rationale was already strong but added detail on cross-tool consistency and automated checking benefits.

rules.statement: Prefer the classic two-process FSM style: a registered state block plus a combinational next-state/output block whose first action is state_d = state_q plus full output defaults — this minimizes latch risk and surfaces missing transitions.

prevents.mechanism: Two-process style with state defaults prevents missed transitions and partial assignments that synthesize as latches.

rules.rationale: Cummings' FSM guidance and lowRISC's style both converge on keeping the sequential state register simple and putting transition logic in a block where defaults are explicit. Verilator's latch and case warnings become much more actionable when this structure is present.

citations.title: State Machine Coding Styles for Synthesis

history.notes: Original rule had good statement and rationale with strong citations. Added violating example showing missing state_d default and incomplete case (STOP state missing), which causes latch inference. Compliant example demonstrates state_d = state_q default at block start plus explicit output defaults. Added two additional prevents entries for simulation coverage and case-incomplete lint warnings to complement existing synth latch prevention.

rules.statement: One-hot FSMs must keep legal-state assumptions visible and checkable; do not combine 'unique case' with a default arm for illegal-state recovery—synthesis optimizes around the unique assumption, making 'default to IDLE' unreliable. Instead, use explicit state-variable defaults and runtime assertions to detect illegal states.

prevents.mechanism: Visible legal-state assertions and absence of unique-case-dependent recovery prevent parasitic exit logic and SEU-recovery surprises.

rules.rationale: VerilogPro warns that unique case can override 'default to IDLE' recovery expectations in one-hot code. Cummings' unique/priority guidance explains why: the keyword communicates strong assumptions to synthesis, not just to the simulator. The 'unique case' keyword tells synthesis that all reachable cases are explicitly listed, allowing aggressive optimization that may eliminate the default arm entirely. In one-hot FSMs, illegal states (multiple bits set, or all-zero) should be caught by assertions rather than silently recovered, since synthesis may not preserve the recovery path. Explicit state-variable initialization before the case statement ensures predictable behavior while maintaining visibility of illegal-state handling.

citations.title: A Solution to Verilog's full_case parallel_case Evil Twins

history.notes: Original rule had strong statement and rationale but lacked concrete code examples showing the unique-case-with-default antipattern. Added violating example demonstrating the problematic pattern and compliant example showing explicit state defaults with assertions. Added predictions for lint metrics (case completeness, full_case pragmas) and FSM extraction success. The existing prevents entry was already well-specified, so no additions needed.

rules.statement: An FSM must reset into exactly one known legal state and must be checked for unreachable, terminal, and unhandled encodings — 'default catches it' is not the same as proving the state graph is complete.

prevents.mechanism: Eliminating unreachable/sink states ensures FSM coverage closes and avoids stuck-controller silicon bugs.

rules.rationale: Older SNUG FSM literature stresses symbolic state definition, explicit reset state, and complete next-state logic as synthesis-facing concerns. In a modern flow, lint-clean CI makes dead or unhandled state encodings cheap to catch before they become waveform archaeology.

citations.title: State Machine Coding Styles for Synthesis

history.notes: Original rule had good statement, rationale, and predictions but lacked code examples. Added violating example showing unreachable sink state (WAIT has no exit path) and compliant example with complete state graph and explicit default. Added prevents entries for synthesis FSM extraction failure and silicon stuck-controller bugs to strengthen downstream impact justification.

rules.statement: A single-bit CDC control must be registered in the source domain and synchronized in the destination with a 2-FF synchronizer (3-FF when MTBF pressure justifies) — never feed raw combinational control across the boundary.

prevents.mechanism: Source-register + 2-FF synchronizer eliminates metastability propagation and pulse loss at the boundary, removing a class of intermittent silicon failures.

rules.rationale: Cummings' CDC paper is still the practical baseline: source-register first, then 2-FF synchronize, widening to 3-FF only when MTBF math says 2-FF isn't enough. Asynchronous boundaries create fault modes that ordinary RTL simulation under-covers.

citations.title: Clock Domain Crossing CDC Design Verification SystemVerilog
Navigating Reset Domain Crossings to Safety in Complex SoCs

history.notes: Original rule had good statement, rationale, and predicts. Added violating example showing combinational CDC crossing and compliant example with proper source registration + 2-FF sync. Added two prevents entries: one for silicon metastability failures (the primary concern) and one for STA false-path coverage (secondary benefit of proper CDC structure).

rules.statement: Multi-bit data or correlated multi-control CDC crossings must use a protocol-based mechanism (handshake, toggle-plus-data MCP formalism, Gray coding with atomic synchronization, or async FIFO) — never synchronize each bit independently as if the signals were unrelated single-bit flags, because inter-bit skew causes data corruption.

prevents.mechanism: Protocol-based multi-bit CDC prevents skewed bus capture, false full/empty conditions, and reconvergence bugs that survive bit-level synchronization.

rules.rationale: Cummings gives concrete failure cases for independently synchronized multi-bit controls and the safe alternatives â consolidation, MCP, Gray code, FIFO. The shift-left survey reinforces that early structural CDC checking belongs in RTL practice. Independent bit-level synchronization of multi-bit buses introduces metastability-induced skew: different bits may resolve in different clock cycles, creating transient invalid values. For Gray-coded pointers, even a single-bit skew produces pointer corruption. For control bundles (e.g., full/empty flags derived from the same counter), skew creates reconvergence hazards where downstream logic sees inconsistent snapshots.

citations.title: Clock Domain Crossing CDC Design Verification SystemVerilog

history.notes: Original rule had strong statement and rationale but lacked concrete code examples. Added violating example showing bit-by-bit Gray pointer synchronization (common FIFO bug) and compliant example with atomic multi-bit synchronizer using concatenation. Enhanced statement to clarify 'atomic synchronization' requirement. Extended rationale to explain skew mechanism and reconvergence hazards. Added two additional prevents entries for synthesis and STA stages to complement existing sim prevention.

rules.statement: Reset-domain crossing must be treated with the same seriousness as CDC — asynchronous reset release must be synchronized per receiving clock domain, and all paths between unlike reset domains must be reviewed with dedicated RDC methodology and tooling. Direct use of signals from one async reset domain in another without proper synchronization is prohibited.

prevents.mechanism: Per-domain reset synchronization eliminates STA recovery/removal violations and reset-release metastability classes.
Cleaning RDC at RTL avoids ATPG cleanup churn from uncontrolled async reset paths.

rules.rationale: Cummings makes the electrical point: async reset removal is itself an asynchronous event needing synchronization. Siemens extends this to modern SoCs as its own signoff discipline; Tessent shows uncontrolled async resets also degrade testability. RDC violations manifest as metastability during reset release, recovery/removal timing violations in STA, and unpredictable initialization sequences. Modern RDC tools (Meridian RDC, Spyglass RDC) provide automated checking similar to CDC verification. Without RDC discipline, designs exhibit field failures during power-on, brown-out recovery, and partial-reset scenarios that are difficult to reproduce in simulation.

citations.title: Synchronous Resets? Asynchronous Resets? I am so confused...
Navigating Reset Domain Crossings to Safety in Complex SoCs
DFT architectural tips testing of asynchronous sets resets

history.notes: Original rule had strong statement and rationale but lacked concrete code examples showing the violation pattern (direct cross-domain reset usage) and the fix (reset synchronization). Added examples demonstrating async reset crossing between clock domains. Enhanced statement to explicitly prohibit direct cross-domain usage. Added prevents entries for simulation X-propagation and silicon reset failures, which are primary RDC failure modes not covered by the existing STA/DFT prevents.

rules.statement: Avoid hand-rolled derived or gated clocks in RTL for ordinary control purposes; prefer clock-enable semantics — direct logic in the clock path creates glitch, skew, and analysis problems that synthesis tools convert back into enables anyway.

prevents.mechanism: Avoiding hand-gated clocks eliminates skew-driven hold failures and CDC/constraint noise that surface only in STA.

rules.rationale: AMD's guidance is blunt: convert clock gating logic to clock enables when possible because it maps better to clock resources and simplifies timing analysis. Recent writeups from 24x7FPGA and Antmicro show why ad-hoc logic is brittle and why modern flows prefer ICG insertion or auto-gating.

citations.title: Converting Clock Gating to Clock Enable - AMD UG949
Implementing automatic clock gating in OpenROAD

history.notes: Original rule had good statement, rationale, and one predict/prevent entry. Added violating example showing combinational clock gating (clk & enable) and compliant example using clock-enable in sequential block. Extended prevents to cover synthesis elaboration, simulation races, power optimization, and DFT controllability issues that hand-gated clocks introduce across the flow.

rules.statement: Design RTL must stay inside the standard synthesizable subset; exclude behavioral conveniences like timing delays (#delay) in RTL processes, force/release statements, fork...join concurrency, or initial blocks with non-constant assignments that belong in verification code.

prevents.mechanism: Restricting RTL to the synthesizable subset prevents tool-specific elaboration failures and silent semantic divergence between simulators and synthesizers.

rules.rationale: Yosys recommends limiting new designs to standardized always forms because those are the cases all synthesis tools agree on. Verilator's warnings around delayed procedural constructs and forked lifetimes underscore that even 'just for convenience' syntax pushes code out of the safe portable subset. Non-synthesizable constructs create a semantic gap between simulation and synthesis: timing delays are ignored by synthesis tools, fork...join creates parallel threads that have no hardware equivalent, and force/release bypasses normal signal assignment semantics. This divergence leads to RTL that simulates correctly but synthesizes incorrectly, or fails elaboration entirely in synthesis tools.

history.notes: Added concrete violating and compliant examples showing timing delay usage. Enhanced statement to explicitly list #delay, initial blocks, and force/release. Added prevents entries for simulation divergence issues (X-propagation, race conditions) that complement the existing synthesis elaboration failure prevention. The rule already had good predicts entries covering rtl.lint.nonsynth.count and synth.warnings.elab_failure.

rules.statement: Synthesizable RTL must not read or write internal signals of another module via hierarchical references; cross-module connectivity must be expressed through ports, interfaces, or package-visible constants.

prevents.mechanism: Eliminating hierarchical references avoids HIERPARAM-class elaboration errors and hierarchy-coupled ECO pain across tools.

rules.rationale: lowRISC flatly prohibits hierarchical references in synthesizable RTL because they undermine modularity and tool compatibility. Verilator's HIERPARAM error shows the same issue at elaboration: these shortcuts quickly become impossible to support consistently.

history.notes: Added violating and compliant examples showing hierarchical reference violations (both read and write) and the correct port-based approach. The rule already has strong predictions (rtl.lint.hierref.count = 0) and prevents (synth elaboration failures). Statement and rationale are clear and well-cited. Quality score 8 reflects a well-formed rule that only lacked concrete code examples.

rules.statement: Module configurability must use typed parameter/localparam or package constants rather than design-shaping `define macros or defparam — elaboration-time parameters are visible to tools and review while preprocessor state is global, textual, and easy to misuse.

prevents.mechanism: Typed parameterization makes build variants traceable and reproducible at synth time, eliminating hidden global preprocessor dependencies.

rules.rationale: lowRISC's style guide is explicit that `define should never parameterize a module and that typed parameters are preferred for safety and readability. Mainstream 2025 explanatory material still emphasizes the same point â the failure mode remains common. Using `define for configuration creates hidden dependencies that are not visible in module interfaces, making it difficult to trace configuration variants during synthesis and verification. Typed parameters provide compile-time type checking, are scoped to the module hierarchy, and are properly handled by all modern EDA tools including formal verification and synthesis tools. The defparam construct, while legal SystemVerilog, bypasses the module interface and creates maintenance hazards by allowing external override of internal constants.

citations.title: Understanding define parameter localparam in SystemVerilog

history.notes: Original rule had good statement and rationale with proper citations. Added realistic violating/compliant examples showing `define vs parameter usage in a FIFO controller context. Enhanced rationale to explain type checking and scoping benefits. Added prevents entries for sim race conditions and DFT visibility issues that complement the existing synth elaboration prevention.

rules.statement: Long register-to-register combinational depth at RTL is an early timing-debt signal: deeper stages tend to correlate with worse downstream WNS/TNS unless later pipelined or rebalanced. Modules with max_comb_depth > 12 levels at target frequencies above 500 MHz should be flagged for re-pipelining review.

prevents.mechanism: Detecting deep comb chains at RTL flags re-pipelining work before synth, avoiding a costly STA-driven re-RTL cycle.

rules.rationale: RTL-Timer reports correlation above 0.89 with post-optimization WNS/TNS gains, strong evidence that structural timing signals are present in RTL. RTLDistil, MasterRTL, and the 2025 shift-left survey all reinforce that timing is now a predictable RTL-stage property. Deep combinational paths create timing pressure that compounds through synthesis optimization, placement, and routing. Early detection enables architectural fixes (pipelining, operator rebalancing) that are 10-100x cheaper than post-STA ECOs. The 12-level threshold is calibrated for modern 7nm-class processes at GHz frequencies; lower-frequency designs may tolerate deeper logic.

citations.title: Annotating Slack Directly on Your Verilog
Bridging Layout and RTL Knowledge Distillation Timing Prediction

history.notes: Original rule had strong rationale and citations but lacked concrete code examples. Added realistic 18-level violating datapath and 3-stage pipelined compliant version. Extended predictions to include TNS, failing endpoints, and area impact. Added prevents entries for synthesis area inflation and PnR congestion. Clarified statement with explicit threshold context.

rules.statement: Modules with control nets exceeding 64 fanout, combinational mux cones deeper than 8 levels, or disproportionate hierarchy fan-in/fan-out ratios (>10:1) should be flagged at RTL as congestion candidates. Commercial physically-aware tools and open flows surface congestion, max-fanout, and hierarchy hotspots as meaningful pre-route indicators that correlate with routing stress.

prevents.mechanism: RTL-stage fanout and hot-hierarchy flags let floorplanning and buffering address congestion before route, saving PnR iterations.
Early fanout limits reduce post-route hold-fix detours and buffering explosion.

rules.rationale: RTL Architect explicitly aggregates timing, power, and congestion by hierarchy and source line, an endorsement that RTL structure predicts physical stress points. AMD and OpenROAD document the same downstream symptoms via congested regions, top modules, and max-fanout metrics. High-fanout control signals create routing bottlenecks that manifest as congestion hotspots during place-and-route. The 64-fanout threshold aligns with typical buffer insertion policies and represents the point where manual RTL restructuring (hierarchical enables, pipelining, or partitioning) becomes more effective than post-synthesis buffering alone. Large mux trees similarly predict congestion by concentrating routing demand in localized regions.

citations.title: Optimizing the RTL Design Flow with Real-Time PPA Analysis

history.notes: Original rule had good rationale and prevents entries but lacked concrete code examples. Added violating example showing 128-bit fanout on single enable signal and compliant example demonstrating hierarchical buffering strategy. Improved statement to include specific numeric thresholds (64 fanout, 8-level mux depth, 10:1 hierarchy ratio) for actionable checking. Added predicts entries linking RTL fanout metrics to downstream congestion and timing failures with realistic numeric ranges based on industry experience.

rules.statement: RTL register bits (total bits in always_ff or @(posedge clk) registers) serve as a first-order proxy for post-synthesis flip-flop count, typically correlating within 0.8x-1.2x. The multiplier deviates when synthesis applies retiming, shift-register extraction (SRL), RAM inference from register arrays, register merging, or constant propagation. For standard pipeline/datapath logic without these optimizations, the correlation is near-linear.

prevents.mechanism: Calibrated FF-count proxy at RTL lets early area/power budgeting be reliable enough to drive architectural decisions.

rules.rationale: PRIMAL states a one-to-one correspondence between RTL and gate-level registers as the basis for learning power from RTL traces â empirical justification for using register-bit count as a first-order resource metric. CircuitFusion and synthesis options like retiming/SRL extraction explain why this stays a heuristic. In practice, designs with deep shift registers (>16 stages) or large register arrays may see 4x-16x compression via SRL primitives or memory inference. Conversely, retiming can increase FF count by 10-20% to improve timing. Clock-gating insertion does not change FF count but adds ICG cells. For early estimation, assume 1.0x for control logic and 0.5x-0.8x for designs with significant shift-register or FIFO structures.

citations.title: PRIMAL Power Inference using Machine Learning

history.notes: Original rule had solid rationale and citations but lacked concrete code examples showing when the proxy holds vs. breaks. Added violating example (shift register with SRL extraction) and compliant example (simple pipeline). Enhanced statement to quantify typical correlation range (0.8x-1.2x) and added rationale detail on compression factors. Added prevents entries for STA and power to show architectural value of early FF estimation.

rules.statement: Arrays that are logically memories must be coded in inference-friendly templates whose read/write semantics match the desired implementation. Specifically: (1) synchronous reads enable block RAM inference, (2) asynchronous reads force distributed/LUT RAM or FF-RAM, (3) port patterns (1R1W, 2RW, etc.) must match target memory primitives, and (4) size thresholds determine whether synthesis maps to dedicated block RAM vs distributed resources. Explicitly match your template to the intended memory type.

prevents.mechanism: Inference-template match drives correct memory implementation choice at synth, avoiding accidental FF-RAM blowups or missed BRAM inference.
Correct memory inference can swing area by orders of magnitude vs FF-RAM fallback; making the template explicit prevents that surprise.

rules.rationale: Yosys is unusually clear that asynchronous reads force LUT/FF-style memory while synchronous-read, supported-port templates can become block RAM. AMD documents the same behavior via RAM_STYLE and the sync-vs-async distinction between dedicated block RAM and distributed RAM. The distinction is critical for resource utilization: a 256×8 memory with asynchronous read will consume ~2048 LUTs or FFs, while the same memory with synchronous read can map to a single block RAM primitive. Synthesis tools use read synchronicity as the primary discriminator because block RAM primitives have registered outputs. Violating this rule typically results in 10-100× area inflation and may cause routing congestion or timing closure failure.

citations.title: Choosing Between Distributed RAM and Block RAM

history.notes: Original rule had solid statement and prevents but lacked code examples. Added violating example showing async read (common mistake that prevents BRAM inference) and compliant example with synchronous read. Enhanced statement to enumerate the four key template dimensions (read sync, port pattern, size). Added rationale quantifying the resource impact (2048 LUTs vs 1 BRAM) to emphasize severity. Predicts and prevents were already well-defined.

rules.statement: Wide arithmetic operators (>16 bits) must be budgeted together with target throughput and pipeline depth — synthesis may infer DSPs or large logic structures based on width and timing pressure, and pipeline depth visibly trades area for speed. Designers must explicitly plan operator width, target frequency, and pipeline stages as a unified datapath budget.

prevents.mechanism: Explicit width/pipeline budgeting at RTL guides DSP inference and prevents accidentally oversized datapaths or under-pipelined multiply stages.
Matching pipeline depth to operator width keeps the critical path inside the target period.

rules.rationale: AMD explicitly says multipliers and MAC forms are DSP-inference candidates subject to timing thresholds, so width and performance target both shape the final implementation. Ibex's 1- vs 3-cycle multiplier and writeback-stage configurations are concrete public examples of the same tradeoff. Without explicit budgeting, a 32×32 multiplier may synthesize as pure logic (consuming ~1000 LUTs) or as a DSP block depending on timing constraints, and adding pipeline stages can reduce critical path by 50% while increasing area by 20%. The tradeoff is design-specific and must be intentional rather than emergent.

rules.statement: Idle behavior must be expressed as stable register enables or shared low-activity guard conditions in functional RTL — modern power-estimation and auto-gating flows look for exactly those patterns to estimate clock/data-path/glitch power and prove gating safety. Avoid burying enable logic in ternary operators or complex conditionals; extract enable signals as named nets with clear activity profiles.

prevents.mechanism: Exposing enable structure at RTL makes ICG insertion candidates discoverable by synth, capturing dynamic-power savings instead of leaving them buried.
Clean enable visibility lets power tools quantify clock-gating savings before late optimization.

rules.rationale: PrimePower RTL explicitly explores clock-gating, memory, datapath, and glitch power from RTL â enable visibility is part of the analyzable design intent. Antmicro's OpenROAD gating work makes the structural requirement concrete: candidate nets must be related, low-activity, and SAT-checkable as correct gating conditions. Synthesis tools identify clock-gating candidates by analyzing enable signal activity and proving functional equivalence. When enable logic is buried in ternary operators (e.g., 'q <= en ? d : q') or complex conditionals, the enable structure is obscured, reducing the tool's ability to extract and verify gating opportunities. Explicit enable signals also improve power estimation accuracy at RTL by making activity correlation visible to switching-power models.

rules.statement: AI-generated RTL must be rejected if it invents primitives not present in the approved library manifest, mixes unsupported Verilog-2001 idioms into a SystemVerilog-only codebase, or emits syntax that no tool in the agreed toolchain can parse. Generation quality improves only when external rule-checking and domain-specific retrieval constrain the model to real, validated constructs.

prevents.mechanism: Resolving AI output against an approved language/library manifest prevents non-compiling RTL and fabricated dependency chains from reaching synth.

rules.rationale: The 2025 error analysis argues retrieval-augmented domain knowledge and rule checking are effective precisely because the base model does not reliably know the RTL environment. OpenTitan and Yosys give a practical alternative: define a narrow supported language/style subset and lint everything against it. Practical experience shows AI models frequently hallucinate module names, port lists, or parameter interfaces that superficially resemble real RTL but fail elaboration. Enforcing validation against a curated library manifest (e.g., OpenTitan's approved cell list or a project-specific primitive catalog) catches these errors before they propagate to synthesis, where debugging costs multiply. Linting tools like Verilator and slang can be extended with custom checks to flag unresolved module references.

rules.rationale: Verilator's UNOPTFLAT is not a DFT checker but is a strong early signal that logic is harder to optimize and reason about. Combinational loops (UNOPTFLAT) prevent reliable scan-chain shifting because the loop state is indeterminate during scan operations. Asynchronous controls that bypass test_mode multiplexing cannot be forced to known states during ATPG, leading to X-propagation and reduced fault coverage. Explicit synchronizers and test-mode overrides ensure all async paths are bounded, reviewable, and controllable during manufacturing test.

rules.statement: The preferred RTL surface for clock-gating inference is the ordinary enable-inside-always_ff pattern — it preserves clean clock semantics while exposing shared conditional update behavior to synthesis and RTL power tools. rules.rationale: PrimePower RTL explicitly explores clock-gating and glitch-power opportunities from RTL, so visible enable structure is part of the analyzable design intent. Manual clock gating creates derived clocks that complicate timing analysis, increase CDC risk, and prevent synthesis tools from optimizing clock-tree insertion. The enable-inside-always_ff pattern allows tools to automatically insert integrated clock-gating (ICG) cells with proper glitch-free control logic, test-mode bypass, and timing-aware placement.

module spi_fsm(

rules.statement: Every single-bit signal crossing from clock domain A to clock domain B must pass through a minimum 2-stage flip-flop synchronizer clocked by the destination domain clock.

prevents.mechanism: 2-FF synchronizer resolves metastability within one clock cycle, preventing random bit flips from propagating to downstream logic.

rules.rationale: Unsynchronized CDC paths are the #1 cause of intermittent silicon failures. Metastability from asynchronous capture can produce arbitrary voltages that propagate as corrupted data.

rules.statement: Multi-bit signals (buses wider than 1 bit) must never cross clock domains through simple 2-FF synchronizers; use Gray-coded encoding, MCP handshake protocols, or asynchronous FIFOs.

prevents.mechanism: Independent bit synchronization of multi-bit buses causes bit-skew: different bits arrive at different cycles, producing values that never existed on the source side.

rules.rationale: A 4-bit counter transitioning from 0111 to 1000 can be sampled as 1111 in the destination domain if bits synchronize at different edges. Gray coding ensures only 1 bit changes per transition.

rules.statement: No combinational logic (gates, muxes, or any logic expressions) is permitted between the first and second stage of a synchronizer chain; the Q output of stage 1 must connect directly to the D input of stage 2 without any intervening logic.

prevents.mechanism: Combinational logic between synchronizer stages can produce glitches that propagate metastable values, defeating the synchronizer's settling time.

rules.rationale: The synchronizer chain relies on a full clock period for metastable resolution. Any combinational logic in the path reduces settling margin and can re-introduce glitches. This violation is particularly dangerous because glitches from combinational logic can create new metastable events or propagate partially-resolved metastability, effectively bypassing the synchronizer's MTBF guarantees. Even simple gates like AND/OR can sample the metastable value at different times, creating logic hazards.

rules.statement: Asynchronous resets must be de-asserted synchronously to the destination clock domain using a reset synchronizer (2-FF chain with async reset on clear pin), preventing metastability on reset release.

prevents.mechanism: Synchronized de-assertion ensures all flops exit reset in the same clock cycle, preventing inconsistent state where some flops remain in reset one cycle longer.

rules.rationale: If reset de-assertion occurs near the active clock edge, some flops may capture the reset-active value while others see reset-inactive, creating an illegal state that persists until the next reset. Without synchronization, the asynchronous reset de-assertion can violate setup/hold timing at the clock edge, causing different flops to exit reset in different cycles. This creates a metastable window where the reset signal itself can glitch, and downstream logic may see inconsistent initial states. The 2-FF synchronizer ensures reset de-assertion is captured cleanly by the destination clock, guaranteeing all flops exit reset together in a known good state.

rules.statement: Reset signals must not be generated from combinational logic (AND/OR of multiple sources) without a registered glitch-free filter stage; use a dedicated reset controller with synchronization.

prevents.mechanism: A glitch on a combinational-derived reset line can assert reset for a fraction of a cycle, corrupting a subset of flops while leaving others unaffected.

rules.rationale: Combinational glitches on reset paths are undetectable by functional simulation but cause real failures in silicon when gate delays create transient pulses. Static timing analysis and functional simulation cannot detect these glitches because they occur within a single clock cycle due to gate-delay mismatches. The glitch duration depends on process, voltage, and temperature variations, making it non-deterministic. A registered reset controller with proper synchronization ensures that reset assertion and deassertion are clean and occur only on clock edges.

rules.statement: A reset signal generated in clock domain A that controls flip-flops in clock domain B must pass through a reset synchronizer (minimum 2-stage synchronizer) clocked by domain B's clock before being used as an asynchronous reset or synchronous reset condition in domain B.

prevents.mechanism: Reset crossing without synchronization causes metastability on reset assertion/de-assertion in the destination domain, identical to CDC metastability.

rules.rationale: Reset domain crossing is a special case of CDC. The same metastability physics apply to reset signals as to data signals. Without synchronization, the reset signal can violate setup/hold timing at the destination domain's flip-flops, causing metastability that may propagate through the design. This is particularly critical during reset de-assertion, where different flops may exit reset at different times due to metastability resolution, leading to functional failures. The synchronizer must itself be reset by the source domain's reset to ensure proper initialization.

rules.statement: The design must contain zero combinational feedback loops; any signal path that feeds back to itself through purely combinational logic without an intervening register is illegal and blocks synthesis.

prevents.mechanism: Synthesis tools error on combinational loops. Oscillation, indeterminate values, and timing analysis failure result from unbroken loops.

rules.rationale: Combinational loops make timing analysis impossible (infinite delay paths) and cause oscillation in hardware. Verilator UNOPTFLAT detects them.

rules.statement: No combinational path between any two flip-flops shall exceed the maximum logic depth threshold (typically 20-30 levels); paths exceeding this will fail timing closure at target frequency.

prevents.mechanism: Deep combinational paths have long propagation delay. If the delay exceeds the clock period minus setup time, the path fails timing.

rules.rationale: Combinational depth is the strongest RTL-level predictor of timing closure difficulty. Each logic level adds ~50-100ps of delay at advanced nodes. Excessive combinational depth forces synthesis tools to use faster (higher-power, larger-area) cells and may still fail timing. Pipelining deep paths by inserting intermediate registers is the primary mitigation. The threshold varies by process node and target frequency: 20 levels at 1GHz+ on 7nm, 30 levels at 200MHz on 28nm.

rules.statement: No single non-clock/non-reset net shall drive more than the tool-configurable fanout limit (typically 16-32 loads) without explicit buffering annotation or buffer tree insertion; high-fanout control signals (enable, select, etc.) must be identified at RTL and marked with synthesis attributes (e.g., (* max_fanout = N *)) to guide buffer insertion.

prevents.mechanism: Excessive fanout causes slow transitions and high capacitance, leading to transition time DRVs and setup violations across many endpoints.

rules.rationale: High-fanout nets without buffering create long wires and slow edges. Buffer tree insertion is cheaper when planned at RTL than fixed at P&R. Control signals such as enables, selects, and valid flags commonly drive many registers or logic gates. Without buffer trees, these nets exhibit high capacitance, slow slew rates, and increased skew, causing transition time design rule violations (DRVs) and setup timing failures. Synthesis tools can automatically insert buffer trees when guided by RTL attributes, but post-P&R fixes are costly and may require floorplan changes. Identifying high-fanout nets during RTL review enables early buffering strategies and more predictable timing closure.

rules.statement: Arithmetic multipliers wider than 16 bits should use dedicated DSP blocks or pipelined implementations. Single-cycle combinational multipliers wider than 32x32 bits will fail timing at most target frequencies (>200MHz). For multipliers 17-32 bits, evaluate timing constraints; for >32 bits, always pipeline or use DSP primitives.

prevents.mechanism: Wide multipliers create deep combinational trees (O(n^2) area, O(n) depth). Pipelining breaks the critical path.

rules.rationale: A 32x32 multiplier is ~2000 gates of combinational logic. Without pipelining, this is a timing closure killer at frequencies above 200MHz. Modern FPGAs provide DSP48/DSP58 blocks optimized for 18x18 or 27x27 multiplications with built-in pipeline registers. ASICs benefit from Booth-encoded or Wallace-tree multipliers with explicit pipeline stages. Without pipelining, a 48x48 multiplier can exceed 5ns combinational delay in typical 28nm processes.

Enhanced statement to clarify thresholds (17-32 bits: evaluate, >32 bits: always pipeline). Added prevents entries for DSP inference and routing congestion. Expanded rationale with FPGA DSP block details and ASIC timing context.

rules.statement: For synthesis tools to infer SRAM, the read operation must be synchronous (output registered on clock edge); asynchronous reads synthesize to flip-flop arrays causing 10-100x area explosion.

prevents.mechanism: Registered reads match SRAM library cell ports. Without registration, synthesis falls back to individual flip-flops, exploding area.

rules.rationale: A 1024x32 memory as flip-flops is ~32K flops. As SRAM macro, it is a single cell. The area difference can make a design unroutable.

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

rules.statement: Any register array larger than 256 bits total (e.g., 32×8 or 16×16) must be mapped to a compiled SRAM macro from the foundry memory compiler, not left for synthesis inference. Arrays ≤256 bits may be inferred as flip-flops if timing and power permit.

prevents.mechanism: SRAM macros are 5-10x denser than flop arrays and consume 3-5x less power. P&R also benefits from having a rectangular hard macro vs. thousands of standard cells.

rules.rationale: Synthesis-inferred register files consume excessive area and power. Foundry SRAM compilers produce optimized, characterized macros with known timing. For arrays >256 bits, synthesis tools will instantiate hundreds or thousands of flip-flops, leading to poor area efficiency, high dynamic power, and routing congestion. SRAM macros provide dedicated read/write ports, predictable timing arcs, and are pre-characterized for hold-time and setup. The 256-bit threshold is a typical industry guideline; consult your foundry's memory compiler datasheet for exact crossover points.

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

Original rule had good statement and rationale but lacked code examples. Added realistic violating example (32×32 regfile inferred as flops) and compliant example (SRAM macro instantiation). Enhanced statement to clarify the ≤256-bit exception. Expanded rationale with quantitative power/area benefits and foundry guidance. Added three prevents entries covering PnR congestion, power, and STA timing closure.

rules.statement: No FSM state shall lack an outgoing transition; every state must have at least one condition under which it transitions to another state or an explicit self-loop with terminal indication.

prevents.mechanism: An FSM entering a deadlock state with no exit transition causes a permanent system hang requiring power cycle.

rules.rationale: FSM deadlocks are among the hardest bugs to debug in silicon because they often depend on rare input sequences that are hard to reproduce. Static analysis tools may not detect all deadlock scenarios, especially when the deadlock depends on specific input sequences. Formal verification or exhaustive state-space exploration is recommended for mission-critical FSMs. The cost of debugging a deadlock in silicon can exceed 100 engineering hours due to the difficulty of reproducing the exact sequence that triggers the condition.

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

Original rule had good statement and existing predicts/prevents. Added realistic violating example showing ERROR state with no exit transition, compliant example with explicit exit path, and additional prevents entries for simulation coverage and reset timing impacts. Enhanced rationale with cost estimation and formal verification recommendation.

rules.statement: The FSM case statement must include a default clause that transitions to a known safe state (typically reset/idle); for one-hot encoding, all non-one-hot bit patterns must recover to the safe state.

prevents.mechanism: SEU (single-event upset) or power glitch can put FSM in an illegal state. Without a default recovery path, the FSM hangs permanently.

rules.rationale: In space, automotive, and safety-critical applications, soft errors can flip state register bits. The FSM must self-recover from any illegal state within one clock cycle.

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

rules.statement: RTL must not contain manual AND/OR gates on clock signals (e.g., assign gated_clk = clk & enable); use register enable conditions and let synthesis infer ICG cells.

prevents.mechanism: Manual clock gating produces glitches (clock pulse truncation or spurious edges) causing setup/hold violations on all downstream flops.

rules.rationale: Library ICG cells use a negative-edge latch to hold the enable, guaranteeing glitch-free gated clocks. Manual AND gates cannot provide this guarantee.

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

rules.statement: All clock gating cells must have a test-mode bypass (scan_enable OR'd with functional enable) so that during scan shift, clocks reach all gated flip-flops. This applies to both hand-instantiated ICG cells and RTL-inferred clock gating logic.

prevents.mechanism: Without test bypass, gated flops cannot be shifted during scan test, causing zero test coverage for those registers.

rules.rationale: DFT sign-off requires >95% stuck-at coverage. Ungated clock paths during scan are a prerequisite for shifting data through scan chains. Clock gating without test bypass creates untestable islands where scan chains cannot shift data, resulting in DFT rule violations and manufacturing test escapes. Industry-standard ICG cells include a test_enable input specifically for this purpose.

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

Original rule had good statement and predictions but lacked code examples. Added realistic violating example showing hand-rolled clock gating without test bypass, and compliant example with scan_enable OR'd into enable path. Enhanced statement to clarify applicability to both hand-instantiated and inferred ICG. Added prevents entries for simulation X-propagation and silicon test escapes. Expanded rationale to explain untestable islands and industry-standard ICG cell design.

rules.statement: All asynchronous set/reset pins on flip-flops must be controllable from a primary input or test-mode control signal during scan test; no async control may be driven solely by uncontrollable internal logic.

prevents.mechanism: Uncontrolled async set/reset can corrupt scan chain data during shift, causing ATPG failure and reduced test coverage.

rules.rationale: ATPG tools need to control async resets to initialize scan chains to known states. Internal-only async controls make scan-based testing impossible for those flops. During scan shift operations, ATPG tools must be able to force async controls to inactive states to prevent corruption of scan chain data. If an async control is driven by internal combinational logic that depends on scan chain contents, it can toggle unpredictably during shift, causing test pattern corruption and making the flip-flop untestable. This directly reduces stuck-at fault coverage and can mask manufacturing defects.

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

Original rule had good statement, predicts, and prevents entries but lacked code examples. Added realistic violating example showing internal logic driving async reset (common AI-generated pattern) and compliant example with test-mode controllability. Enhanced rationale to explain the scan-shift corruption mechanism more clearly. Added silicon-stage prevent entry to capture field impact of test escapes.

rules.statement: Internal tri-state buses must have a test-mode override that forces exactly one driver active during scan testing; all tri-state enable signals must be controllable from primary inputs or dedicated test-mode signals to prevent bus contention.

prevents.mechanism: Bus contention during scan test causes indeterminate values and potential damage from short-circuit currents.

rules.rationale: Tri-state buses are inherently dangerous in scan mode because multiple drivers may be enabled simultaneously. Modern designs avoid internal tri-state entirely. During scan shift operations, the scan chain can place arbitrary values on internal control signals, potentially enabling multiple tri-state drivers simultaneously. This creates bus contention that produces indeterminate logic values (X), corrupts scan data, and can cause excessive current draw. A test-mode override ensures deterministic single-driver behavior. Best practice is to avoid internal tri-state buses entirely in modern designs, using muxes instead.

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

Original rule had good predictions and prevents but lacked concrete code examples. Added violating example showing tri-state bus without test-mode control, and compliant example with test_mode override forcing safe selection. Enhanced rationale to explain scan-shift hazard mechanism. Added prevents entries for DFT coverage and X-propagation impacts. Clarified statement to emphasize 'exactly one driver active' requirement.

rules.statement: The full_case directive (// synopsys full_case or (* full_case *)) must not be used; it creates simulation/synthesis mismatch by treating unspecified cases as don't-care in synthesis but latches in simulation. Use SystemVerilog unique case instead.

prevents.mechanism: Pre-synthesis simulation shows latch behavior for unspecified cases, but post-synthesis hardware produces arbitrary values. Gate-level equivalence check fails.

rules.rationale: Cummings (SNUG99) documented full_case and parallel_case as 'the evil twins of Verilog synthesis'. They are the most common source of simulation/synthesis mismatch.

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

rules.statement: The parallel_case directive must not be used; it tells synthesis to implement non-priority mux logic, but simulation evaluates as priority-encoded, causing mismatch when case items overlap. Use SystemVerilog unique case instead.

prevents.mechanism: Priority vs. parallel logic mismatch between simulation and synthesis causes functional bugs visible only in silicon.

rules.rationale: parallel_case is the second 'evil twin' (Cummings SNUG99). When case items overlap (common with casex/casez), simulation picks the first match but synthesis treats all as simultaneous. The mismatch is particularly dangerous with casez/casex where overlapping patterns are common (e.g., 4'b1??? and 4'b?1?? both match 4'b1100). Simulation will always pick the first match (priority), while synthesis with parallel_case assumes mutual exclusivity and may optimize to parallel logic, leading to different behavior in silicon. The unique keyword provides compile-time checking that cases are truly non-overlapping and generates the same priority logic in both simulation and synthesis.

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

Original rule had good statement and rationale referencing Cummings SNUG99. Added realistic violating/compliant examples showing casez arbiter pattern where parallel_case creates sim/synth mismatch. Enhanced rationale with concrete example of overlapping patterns. Added prevents entries for sim X-propagation and silicon functional bugs. The predicts entry was already correct (checking rtl.lint.full_case_pragma.count = 0).

rules.statement: All assignments in always_ff or always @(posedge clk) blocks must use non-blocking assignments (<=); blocking assignments (=) create race conditions where simulation evaluation order determines results, but synthesis produces deterministic register hardware.

prevents.mechanism: Blocking assignments in sequential blocks allow data to 'leak' through multiple registers in one delta cycle in simulation, but hardware always takes one clock cycle per register stage.

rules.rationale: This is the single most important Verilog coding rule. Violation causes non-deterministic simulation that may accidentally match hardware behavior on one simulator but fail on another.

violating:
always_ff @(posedge clk) begin
  b = a;  // VIOLATION: blocking in sequential
  c = b;  // c gets a's value this cycle (race!)
end
compliant:
always_ff @(posedge clk) begin
  b <= a;  // non-blocking: correct pipeline
  c <= b;  // c gets b's OLD value (1-cycle delay)
end

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

rules.statement: In always @(...) combinational blocks, every signal read in the block must be in the sensitivity list; use always_comb or always @(*) to auto-generate complete lists and prevent simulation/synthesis mismatch.

prevents.mechanism: Incomplete sensitivity lists cause simulation to miss input-change events that hardware (gates) responds to immediately, creating pre-synthesis vs. post-synthesis behavioral divergence.

rules.rationale: always_comb is the fix: it auto-generates a complete sensitivity list and additionally checks for latch inference. There is no reason to use always @(...) in modern SV. Incomplete sensitivity lists create a class of bugs where RTL simulation produces different results than gate-level simulation or silicon. In the violating example, changes to 'op' will not trigger re-evaluation during RTL simulation, but the synthesized hardware will respond immediately to 'op' changes, causing functional divergence. Modern linters (Verilator COMBDLY, slang) flag these issues, and synthesis tools may infer unintended latches when outputs are not assigned on all control paths that the simulator never exercises due to the incomplete sensitivity list.

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

Original rule had good statement and rationale but lacked code examples. Added violating example showing missing 'op' signal in sensitivity list, causing simulation/synthesis mismatch. Added compliant example using always_comb. Extended rationale to explain the specific failure mechanism and linter detection. Added prevents entries for race conditions and synthesis latch warnings that stem from incomplete sensitivity lists.

rules.statement: Synthesizable RTL must not assign X values (1'bx, 'x, '{default:'x}); synthesis treats X as don't-care and optimizes freely, while simulation propagates X as unknown, creating a fundamental semantic gap.

prevents.mechanism: X-assignments let synthesis pick 0 or 1 for optimization, masking bugs that X-propagation in simulation would have caught. Silicon has the bug that simulation appeared to detect.

rules.rationale: The X-optimism vs. X-pessimism debate is resolved by not using X in synthesizable code. Use explicit default values instead.

violating:
always_comb begin
  case (state)
    IDLE: out = 1'b1;
    RUN:  out = 1'b0;
    default: out = 1'bx;  // VIOLATION: X in synth code
  endcase
end
compliant:
always_comb begin
  case (state)
    IDLE: out = 1'b1;
    RUN:  out = 1'b0;
    default: out = 1'b0;  // explicit safe value
  endcase
end

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

rules.statement: All #delay constructs must be removed from synthesizable RTL; synthesis tools ignore timing delays but simulators honor them, causing behavioral mismatches between pre-synthesis RTL simulation and post-synthesis gate-level simulation that can mask critical timing bugs.

prevents.mechanism: Delays in RTL create false confidence in timing behavior. Gate-level simulation shows different timing than RTL simulation.

rules.rationale: Synthesis strips all #delays. Code that depends on delays for correctness (e.g., #1 to avoid races) masks real bugs that will appear in hardware. Delays are appropriate only in testbenches and timing models. Using delays to work around race conditions or setup/hold issues in RTL indicates a fundamental design flaw that must be fixed with proper synchronous design techniques (e.g., pipeline stages, valid/ready handshakes, or clock-domain crossing protocols).

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

Original rule had good predicts and one prevents entry. Added violating/compliant examples showing delay in sequential logic. Enhanced statement for clarity on sim/synth mismatch. Added rationale about proper alternatives to delays. Added two more prevents entries covering X-propagation and timing closure issues that delays can mask.

rules.statement: initial blocks must not be used for state initialization in ASIC designs (they are not synthesizable); all initialization must be through reset logic. Exception: FPGA flows where initial maps to configuration bitstream.

prevents.mechanism: Simulation starts with initialized values from initial blocks, but ASIC silicon starts with random state. Power-on behavior will differ from simulation.

rules.rationale: initial blocks give false confidence: simulation always passes because state starts correct. ASIC hardware powers up to random values and will fail unpredictably. This creates a simulation-silicon mismatch: testbenches pass because initial blocks execute in simulation, giving predictable starting state. However, ASIC flip-flops power up to random values (determined by process variation, temperature, and voltage). The design will fail in silicon at power-on or after brown-out events, despite clean simulation results. Reset logic must explicitly initialize all state to ensure deterministic behavior in hardware.

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

Original rule had good statement, rationale, and predictions. Added violating/compliant examples showing realistic counter module with initial block vs. reset-only initialization. Enhanced rationale to explain power-on and brown-out failure modes. Added two prevents entries covering synthesis warnings and gate-level X-propagation mismatches that this rule prevents.

rules.statement: casex must not be used in synthesizable code; casez should only be used with explicit don't-care patterns (? or z) on the case items, never on the selector. Prefer SystemVerilog case...inside which has well-defined synthesis semantics and only applies don't-care matching to the item patterns, not the selector expression.

prevents.mechanism: casex treats both X and Z as don't-care in both selector and items, creating match conditions in simulation that differ from synthesized hardware.

rules.rationale: casex is the worst offender: if the selector contains X (e.g., from an uninitialized register), casex matches it as don't-care, hiding the bug. case...inside only applies don't-care to the item patterns. In synthesis, casex and casez with X/Z in the selector can produce hardware that behaves differently from RTL simulation: the simulator may match X as don't-care, while the synthesized logic treats it as a definite 0 or 1. This creates a sim-synth mismatch that is difficult to debug. case...inside enforces that wildcards (?) appear only in the item patterns, ensuring the selector is always fully evaluated.

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

Original rule had good statement and rationale but lacked code examples. Added violating example showing casex with X in pattern (common mistake) and compliant example using case...inside with ? wildcard. Enhanced statement to clarify that casez is acceptable only with explicit don't-care on items. Added prevents entry for sim stage to complement existing synth stage entry, and added synth.warnings.elab_failure as some tools reject these constructs.

rules.statement: Clock and reset signals must not pass through user-defined combinational logic (AND, OR, MUX, XOR, or any other Boolean operators); all clock manipulation must use dedicated library cells (ICG, clock mux, clock divider) and all reset manipulation must use reset synchronizer cells.

prevents.mechanism: Glitches on clock/reset from combinational logic cause spurious edge triggers, corrupting all downstream state. Extremely difficult to debug in silicon.

rules.rationale: Library clock cells (ICGs, muxes, dividers) are designed and characterized to be glitch-free. User RTL combinational logic has no such guarantee. Combinational logic on clocks creates hazards and glitches during input transitions that can cause multiple clock edges or metastability. Clock library cells use latch-based or glitch-free architectures with careful timing characterization. Similarly, reset logic must be synchronized and glitch-free to prevent partial or spurious resets. This rule is critical for both functional correctness and timing closure, as tools cannot properly analyze or optimize user-gated clocks.

history.notes: Added from synthesis-stage RTL design rules research

Original rule was well-structured with good statement and rationale. Added realistic violating/compliant examples showing both clock and reset violations with library cell alternatives. Enhanced rationale to explain glitch mechanisms and timing analysis implications. Added four prevents entries covering STA, simulation, synthesis, and power impacts with specific mechanisms. Slightly refined statement to explicitly mention Boolean operators.

rules.statement: After RTL lint passes cleanly (rtl.lint.latch.count = 0), synthesis must also report zero latches (synth.warnings.latch = 0); any divergence indicates a tool-specific inference gap or a simulation/synthesis mismatch that must be resolved before tape-out.

prevents.mechanism: Eliminates simulation/synthesis mismatch where unintended latches cause state-holding behavior not visible in RTL simulation

rules.rationale: Latches inferred by synthesis but not flagged by lint represent a critical tool-divergence risk: the RTL may simulate correctly but synthesize incorrectly, leading to functional failures in silicon. This rule closes the loop between lint and synthesis to ensure no hidden state elements escape detection.

violating:
// Verilator lint clean, but synth infers latch
always_comb begin
  if (sel) out = a;  // missing else: synth infers latch on 'out'
end

rules.statement: Multi-driver violations reported by synthesis (synth.warnings.multidriven) must be zero when RTL lint (rtl.lint.multidriven.count) is zero; any synthesis-only multi-driver finding indicates a structural elaboration difference that must be debugged and resolved.

prevents.mechanism: Prevents bus contention and X-propagation from undetected multi-driver conflicts that only appear after elaboration

rules.rationale: Multi-driver conflicts are a fundamental correctness violation. If synthesis detects drivers that lint does not, it suggests hierarchical instantiation, generate-block expansion, or parameter-dependent logic created multiple assignment paths invisible to static lint. This must be resolved to prevent X-propagation and functional corruption.

compliant:
// Single driver per net
module top; logic sig; sub u1(.q(sig)); endmodule

rules.statement: Any hold-time violations (sta.hold_violations > 0) in post-synthesis STA must trigger a mandatory CDC/RDC audit: hold failures on cross-domain paths indicate missing or insufficient synchronization, while intra-domain hold failures suggest overly aggressive clock skew or insufficient insertion delay.

prevents.mechanism: Ensures synchronizers have sufficient delay and that intra-domain paths have adequate hold margin before tape-out
Prevents late-stage hold-buffer insertion that causes routing congestion and area bloat

rules.rationale: Hold violations are non-recoverable timing failures that cause metastability and data corruption in silicon. Cross-domain hold failures are especially dangerous because they indicate synchronizer stages are too close or improperly placed. This rule enforces root-cause analysis and structural fixes rather than band-aid hold-fixing.

rules.statement: Any reset recovery or removal violations (sta.reset_recovery_removal_violations > 0) must be resolved before tape-out; these indicate asynchronous reset assertion/deassertion timing failures that cause metastability on the reset path itself, leading to partial or incorrect reset behavior.

prevents.mechanism: Ensures reset deassertion is synchronous and meets timing, preventing partial or metastable reset states

rules.rationale: Reset recovery/removal violations are as critical as hold violations but affect the reset network. A violation means some flip-flops may not reset correctly, leaving the design in an illegal state. This is especially dangerous for FSMs and control logic where partial reset causes deadlock or corruption.

rules.statement: Any detected simulator race conditions (sim.race_condition_count > 0) must be treated as a blocking/non-blocking assignment discipline violation or incomplete sensitivity list; races indicate order-dependent behavior that will diverge between simulators and between simulation and synthesis.

prevents.mechanism: Eliminates order-dependent behavior that causes simulation to pass but silicon to fail, or vice versa

rules.rationale: Race conditions are a symptom of incorrect RTL coding style: mixing blocking and non-blocking in ways that create order dependencies, or using incomplete sensitivity lists in combinational blocks. These cause non-deterministic simulation results and simulation/synthesis mismatches that are extremely difficult to debug in silicon.
